sop.coo_evaluation:
# **Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member complaint & incident escalation** – no documented path for complaints (class quality, coach behaviour, billing disputes, injury), investigation, resolution, or when to involve insurance/legal
- **Churn analysis & win-back campaigns** – no SOP linking cancellation reasons to targeted re-engagement (vs. blanket "we miss you" emails)
- **Coach performance management & corrective action** – no SOP for flagging underperformance (low NPS, member complaints, no-shows), coaching conversations, improvement plans, or termination
- **Financial reconciliation & reporting** – no monthly close process, variance analysis, or reporting cadence to leadership
- **Health & safety incident log** – no SOP for documenting near-misses, injuries, equipment failures, or safety audits
- **Member health screening & informed consent updates** – no SOP for annual health questionnaire refresh, contraindications flagging, or liability sign-offs
- **Staffing handover & knowledge transfer** – no exit SOP when coaches/staff leave (knowledge capture, client reassignments, access revocation)
- **Crisis communication** – no SOP for class cancellations, facility closures, coach departures, or brand-damaging incidents

---

# **Needs updating**

- **Coach onboarding (17/17 exist but likely incomplete)** – check for: brand voice/tone standards, member safety scenarios, de-escalation training, social media policies, confidentiality
- **Class scheduling & capacity** – verify peak-hour overflow protocol, waitlist-to-booking conversion SOP, class cancellation notice periods
- **Member billing & payment failures** – check for: dunning sequence, failed payment follow-up timing, refund/credit policy clarity
- **Client feedback loops** – NPS/survey collection exists but verify: who acts on feedback, response

kpi.advice:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO ACTION PLAN

## REVENUE — BIGGEST PRIORITY

- **AR/M collapsed from £165.56 (May) to £120.48 (June); you've gained 25 members (+20%) but revenue per member fell 27%.** Membership pricing or mix has shifted downward. Audit your June sign-ups: are new members on a lower tier? Raise entry-level pricing by £5–8/month and push higher-tier packages (premium PT add-ons, corporate memberships) at sign-up. Target: restore AR/M to £160+ by August.
- **Churn at 0% in June is excellent but masks a 5.74% quarterly average (May–Jun vs Feb–Apr avg 4.74%).** Implement a 30-day re-engagement email sequence for at-risk members (those attending <2×/week) with a free class or partner referral bonus. Cost: negligible; upside: 2–3 retained members per month = £240–360/month.
- **Off-peak capacity now available post-relocation (doubled studio).** Fill quiet slots (likely early morning/midweek) with budget "off-peak" memberships (£20–25/month, weekday 6am–11am only). Target: 8–12 uptakes = £160–300/month incremental.
- **June revenue down 13% vs May (£20,199 → £17,711) despite 122 → 147 members.** Investigate: are members paying in arrears? AR spike or payment collection lag is eating cash. Enforce upfront payment at sign-up and monthly auto-debit; write off any >60 days overdue.

## EXPENSES — FOCUS AFTER REVENUE FIXED

- **Non-people costs surged May–Jun: £9,233 → £9,390.** Drill into the detail: rent, utilities, supplies, insurance. Post-relocation, larger rent is expected, but June should stabilise. If rent is fixed, isolate utilities, cleaning, kit. June non-people % is 53% of revenue (vs May 46%); target 42–45% by Q3. Renegotiate supplier contracts (cleaning, laundry, equipment maintenance) by July.
- **People costs stable at £3,686 (June); payroll is lean at 21% of revenue.** With 147 members, you may need one part-time receptionist or PT instructor—but only *after* revenue hits £22k+/month. Do not add headcount until AR/M recovers.

##

staff.advice:
• **Move Grace's Build: Lower (17 sessions) to JoJo and Eilis.** JoJo already runs Build: Upper; add Lower to create full Build coverage. This is JoJo's strength and spreads the load. Eilis can support with 6–8 sessions to build her confidence on a non-Tuesday class.

• **Redistribute Grace's Elevate (6) and Strongevity (3) to Eilis.** Eilis has capacity and these aren't Tuesday-dependent. This gives her more class hours and reduces Grace's timetable immediately.

• **Reassign Grace's PT (0 sessions this month, but ongoing risk).** Any new PT enquiries should go to JoJo or Eilis. If Grace has existing PT clients, transition them to JoJo by end of July; JoJo can absorb 5–8 more PT slots without strain given her core class load is stable.

• **Handover Grace's No Sweat Intro (3) to Eilis or JoJo.** These are low-intensity and ideal for Eilis to build rapport with new members. Frees Grace entirely.

• **Reassign all InBody scans (Grace's 9 + JoJo's 20) to Eilis as admin/support task.** They're not coaching; Eilis can do them in downtime and frees both senior coaches for real delivery. Aim for Eilis to own all scans going forward.

• **JoJo is not overloaded at 133 sessions (many are scans).** Once Grace's ~35 class/PT hours shift out, JoJo stays the anchor; Eilis rises to ~55–60 sessions and becomes the second coach. This balances the team and removes founder dependency.

class_stats.suggestion:
**For quiet studio time gaps:**

1. **Add a paid "Open Lift" block on Monday and Thursday evenings** — those are your quietest days with likely gaps between classes. Charge £5-8 per session for unsupervised barbell access; appeals to members who want flexibility without a full class commitment and generates revenue from dead time.

2. **Run a 30-min "Mobility + Coffee" workshop on Saturday mornings before or after the quietest period** — Saturday is your weakest day overall (203 attendees in 90 days). A low-intensity, social slot with tea/coffee included costs little to run and builds Saturday habit and community.

**For the quieter classes themselves:**

3. **Move "Engine" and "Capacity" to Tuesday or Wednesday mornings (6-7am slot if available) and send targeted invites to your "Build" class regulars** — your busiest days suggest a strong weekday morning crowd. Engine and Capacity are conditioning work that complements strength training; reposition them as pre-work finishers for the Build audience who already trust your programming, rather than standalone classes fighting for attention on quiet days.

kpis.Action: INTERNAL: Create clear service menu (for coaches and for members) — what we offer, what it costs, what's included.
kpis.advice:
# Service Menu Execution for The Forge

**1. Audit what you're ACTUALLY selling right now**
Don't start from scratch. Spend 2 hours this week asking your top 3 coaches: "What do members most frequently ask about?" and "What do we keep explaining the same way?" Your menu should reflect real demand, not theoretical offerings. This becomes your baseline.

**2. Price-test before you publish**
Don't guess. Grab 5-10 current members this week for a 10-minute chat: "If we offered [service X] at £Y/month, would you buy it?" Their hesitation or enthusiasm tells you if your pricing is off. Do this before finalizing anything public.

**3. Build TWO separate menus with different language**
Coach menu = operational (class capacity, what setup they need, time blocks). Member menu = benefits-focused (what transformation they get, social proof, limited spots to create urgency). Don't make coaches read sales copy, and don't make members read logistics.

**4. Post it in THREE places, test which converts**
Website, printed poster in gym, WhatsApp/email newsletter. After 2 weeks, track which drove inquiries. Ruthlessly cut the non-performers. Your menu isn't fixed—it evolves based on what actually makes the phone ring.

**5. Build a "start here" path for confused prospects**
Most won't know what they need. Add a simple decision tree or call-to-action ("Not sure? Try a £15 induction session first") that funnels fence-sitters into your highest-margin offering.

What's your current pricing structure like—is it membership-only right now?

kpis.Action: EXTERNAL: Refine our funnel landing pages to make it very clear what we offer, look at common questions
kpis.advice:
# Landing Page Refinement — Action Plan for Grace

• **Audit your current drop-off points first** — Pull analytics on your existing landing pages. Where do visitors bounce? What pages convert best? Start there, not from scratch. You're refincing, not rebuilding.

• **Mine your actual FAQ source material** — Don't guess. Pull questions from: recent sales calls (what do prospects ask?), your Instagram DMs, email inquiries, and front-desk staff conversations. These are real friction points. Document 15-20 of them.

• **Create one "clarity hierarchy" per page** — Reorder content so the first 3 seconds answer: "Who is this for?" (women only), "What problem do you solve?" (strength, community, confidence — pick YOUR main one), "What's different here?" (vs. other gyms). One clear value prop wins over comprehensive.

• **A/B test the critical conversion moment** — Don't guess what drives signups. Test: headline variations (benefit-led vs. curiosity-led), CTA button text/color, and whether showing member testimonials or program details converts better. Run for 2 weeks minimum.

• **Build a "reasons we're different" section addressing Belfast-specific context** — Are you the only women-only gym in the city? Emphasize that. Address common objections you actually hear (price, intimidation factor, schedule). Answer them directly on the page.

**Start with the FAQ audit this week.** Everything else flows from real questions.

kpis.Action: Implement internal member feedback system (simple monthly form or post-class check-in)
kpis.advice:
# Implement Member Feedback System — Concrete Execution for The Forge

• **Start with post-class check-ins, not forms.** Train your instructors to ask 2 questions during cool-down: "What worked for you today?" and "One thing we could do better?" Takes 90 seconds, feels natural, builds rapport. Capture notes in your phone immediately after. You'll get honest feedback without the friction of "filling out a form."

• **Make it a competitive advantage tool, not a vanity project.** Flag feedback by frequency (e.g., "3 members mentioned wanting earlier morning sessions," "2 complained about shower temperature"). Share wins back to members within 2 weeks ("You asked for 6:30am classes — launching next month"). They feel heard; you get real data for decisions.

• **Tie it directly to retention and upsell.** Use feedback to identify at-risk members (complaints signal churn risk) and quick wins (someone mentions wanting nutrition advice = referral partner or paid service opportunity). New members specifically get asked what made them join — that's gold for your marketing message.

• **Systemize without technology overhead.** Use a simple Google Sheet or even a WhatsApp group note — don't overthink it. One column: date/member name, feedback, action taken. Review monthly. Overcomplicated systems die. Simple ones become habit.

• **Close the loop visibly.** When a member's feedback leads to action, tell *them* specifically ("Remember you mentioned the music was too loud? We've switched playlist curators"). This converts one person's complaint into proof that The Forge listens.

kpis.Action: Write SOP's for membership members, personal training members, fuel forward, well75, strongevity. Every service should have a clear SOP.
kpis.advice:
# SOP Execution Strategy for The Forge

**1. Reverse-engineer from pain points, not blank pages**
Don't start writing. Spend 2 hours this week shadowing your best staff member during peak hours. Document what actually confuses members or trips up your team (late arrivals? billing questions? fuel forward ordering?). Write SOPs solving *real problems*, not theoretical ones. This makes them sticky and used.

**2. Create one "master SOP template" with 4 fixed sections: Member Journey → Payment/Logistics → Touchpoints with Staff → Troubleshooting**
Use this template identically across all five services. Members learn the format once. Your team has consistency to train new hires against. Takes 30 mins to template, saves 20 hours on writing.

**3. Test each SOP with a non-expert first**
Before finalizing, give the membership SOP to someone new on your team (or a friend unfamiliar with The Forge). If they get stuck, rewrite that section. This catches jargon and assumed knowledge that kills adoption.

**4. Assign one owner per service + give them 3-day turnaround**
You write membership. Assign fuel forward to whoever runs that product. Strongevity to whoever manages those members. They know the real friction. You quality-check and standardize the template—don't write five SOPs solo.

**5. Host a 20-minute "SOP launch" with your team**
Walk through one completed SOP together. Ask: "What's missing? What would help you actually use this?" Iterate once. Now they own it, not you.

**Output: Five one-page SOPs, not 10

kpis.Action: Establish a consistent social media content calendar with 3–4 posts/week planned in advance
kpis.advice:
# Social Media Content Calendar: The Forge Execution Plan

**Build it in 90 minutes, not weeks:**
Use a free Google Sheet template (search "content calendar template") — columns: Date | Platform | Content Type | Hook | Image/Video | Post Time. Don't overthink the design. Start with next 4 weeks only. Perfectionism kills momentum.

**Populate with what actually converts for women's fitness:**
- **Monday/Thursday**: Before-after transformations or member spotlights (builds community, drives referrals)
- **Wednesday**: Quick workout tips or myth-busting (positions Grace as expert, shareable)
- **Saturday**: Behind-the-scenes (new equipment, cleaning day, staff banter) — humanizes the brand
Assign each to Instagram, TikTok, or Facebook based on where your current members already are.

**Source content ruthlessly from existing assets:**
Stop creating from scratch. Film 6-8 short clips during a single gym session (members doing movements, class energy, Grace coaching) and batch-edit into 4 weeks of posts. Repurpose the same clip with different captions for different platforms.

**Automate the scheduling NOW:**
Use Buffer or Later (free tier works) — schedule all 16 posts Sunday evening for the month. Takes 20 minutes. This removes the "remember to post" friction that kills consistency.

**Assign accountability:**
If Grace won't personally post, delegate to a member or staff member for 30 mins/week with a simple brief document. Consistency beats perfection.

What's your current posting frequency right now?

kpis.Action: Schedule regular videography sessions every 8 weeks with Jordan
kpis.advice:
# Executing Videography Sessions with Jordan

**Lock the calendar NOW** – Book all 8-week slots for the next 12 months back-to-back with Jordan today. This removes the "scheduling friction" that kills consistency. One admin task, done.

**Define the content output upfront** – Agree with Jordan on deliverables *per session*: e.g., 3-5 short-form clips (Instagram/TikTok), 1 longer testimonial piece, 1 behind-the-scenes reel. Get it in writing. Prevents scope creep and wasted filming time.

**Assign one person as the "content point"** – You or a staff member owns prepping locations, gathering members willing to be filmed, coordinating timing. Jordan shows up to *shoot*, not problem-solve on the day. This cuts session time by 30-40%.

**Build a posting schedule *backwards* from each session** – Before Jordan arrives, map out when those 8 videos drop (e.g., 2 per week for 4 weeks). This forces you to actually *use* the content and keeps momentum between sessions.

**Tie it to a revenue goal** – Decide: these videos exist to drive trial sign-ups, retention messaging, or partnership deals. Track which videos generate inquiries. This tells you whether to adjust filming focus in session #2.

kpis.Action: Revisit systemize automations for 6 week trial enquiries
kpis.advice:
# Automating 6-Week Trial Enquiries for The Forge

• **Audit your current leakage first** — Before building automations, pull the last 30 trial enquiries and map exactly where each one dropped off (no response? scheduled but didn't show? showed but didn't convert?). Your automation should plug *that specific gap*, not just exist.

• **Build a 3-email sequence, not more** — Day 1 (same-day): Welcome + clear trial booking link (make it frictionless). Day 7: Social proof from existing members + class schedule. Day 14: "Last chance" + limited offer (scarcity works on fence-sitters). Automate through your booking platform or Mailchimp — don't manually send.

• **Lock in a trial-to-membership conversion milestone** — The automation should trigger a *different* sequence if they book the trial. During that 6 weeks, send 2-3 touchpoints: Week 2 (mid-trial check-in), Week 5 (membership offer). Without this, you're automating enquiries into a void.

• **Set one metric to track ruthlessly** — Trial-to-booking rate, then booking-to-show rate, then show-to-convert rate. Pick the worst performer and that's where your next tweak goes. Review weekly for 2 weeks, then monthly.

• **Test with SMS if email's stalling** — If your email open rates are under 25%, send a text on day 1 ("Grace here — got your enquiry, here's your trial link"). Texts hit 98% open rate. Use Twilio or your booking platform's SMS feature.

What's

kpis.Action: Finalise and document weekly action checklist for each role (JoJo, Eilis, Sarah) with KPIs they own
kpis.advice:
# Weekly Action Checklist Execution for The Forge

**1. Role-Specific KPI Audit First**
Don't start writing checklists. Spend 90 minutes with each person separately asking: "What are the 3-5 things that, if you nail them weekly, make The Forge money or keep members?" Document their answers verbatim. This surfaces what they actually control vs. what's noise.

**2. Build the Checklist Around Revenue Levers**
For each role, anchor tasks to ONE clear KPI:
- **JoJo** (assume Operations): Member retention rate + class utilization % → checklist tasks: weekly churn follow-ups, class scheduling optimization
- **Eilis/Sarah** (assume Sales/Community): New member sign-ups + class attendance → checklist tasks: referral outreach, first-timer onboarding calls

Map 5-7 weekly actions *directly* to each KPI, not general to-dos.

**3. Make It Non-Negotiable & Visible**
Print A3 posters, laminate, put them at their station. Include: the weekly action, due day/time, how success looks numerically (not "do good follow-ups"—"complete 8 retention calls by Thursday"). Track completion daily in a shared Slack channel or WhatsApp group.

**4. Lock in a 15-Min Friday Sync**
Each Friday 4pm: Grace + person reviews *actual numbers* vs. KPI target, flags blockers (staffing, systems, clarity). This prevents checklists becoming theatre.

**5. Version 1.0 by End of Week**
Don't overthink. Get rough

kpis.Action: Run team vision session — share the SSO, 90-day plan, and each person's lane clearly
kpis.advice:
# Vision Session Execution for The Forge

**Before the session (do this today):**
- Create a **one-page visual** of your SSO (Single Shared Objective) + 90-day milestones — print it. People retain 65% more from visual + verbal. Don't read from slides; use it as a prop while you talk conviction into the room.
- Map each team member's **specific deliverables** (not job titles) — e.g., "Sarah owns member retention metrics + class scheduling" not "Sarah does operations." Be ruthless about who owns what metric.

**During the session (structure matters):**
- **Start with why it matters to them personally** — "If we hit this 90-day target, we can afford X/Y/Z you've asked for" or expanded shifts, bonuses, whatever. You need emotional buy-in, not just intellectual agreement.
- **Go lane-by-lane, not top-down** — have each person articulate back what success looks like in their lane in 90 days. If they can't articulate it clearly, you haven't been specific enough. Fix it right there.

**Seal commitment:**
- End with a **public commitment** — each person states one thing they're accountable for delivering in the next 30 days. This isn't motivational; it's mechanical. Public commitment drives follow-through 3x better than private.

When's your session? I can tighten the SSO wording if you want to share it.

kpis.Action: Audit workload distribution — identify who is over-stretched and rebalance over first 30 days
kpis.advice:
# Workload Audit for The Forge — 30-Day Rebalance

• **Map it in hours, not vibes.** Have each team member (instructors, front desk, admin) log actual hours spent on: classes/sessions, member check-ins, cleaning/maintenance, admin tasks, and ad-hoc requests. Use a simple shared spreadsheet for 2 weeks. This reveals where time actually goes vs. where Grace *thinks* it goes.

• **Identify the bottleneck person first.** Someone's likely carrying 60% of the load. Ask them directly: "What's the one task you hate that's eating your week?" That's usually the rebalance lever. Often it's admin/scheduling/compliance stuff that could be batched, delegated, or partially automated (e.g., member onboarding checklists handled by front desk instead of instructors).

• **Create 2–3 concrete swaps this week.** Don't rebalance everything at once. Pick 3 specific tasks to reassign immediately—e.g., "Sarah stops writing class schedules, Dan takes it every Monday." Get buy-in by showing them what gets *off* their plate in return, not just what gets added.

• **Run a 1-week trial before locking it in.** Small changes expose what actually works. If a reassignment tanks, you pivot fast instead of discovering this in week 4.

• **Anchor it to one metric that matters to Grace.** Track member satisfaction (quick post-class text poll) and staff stress (quick Friday pulse check). If rebalancing improves both by day 20, she has proof to keep it.

# Cutting 10% Costs at The Forge — Tactical Breakdown

**Energy & Utilities (Quick Win — ~2-3% savings)**
- Audit your gym's peak vs. off-peak usage patterns *this week*. Shift non-essential lighting/HVAC to off-peak hours. Get 3 quotes from energy brokers (not just your current supplier) — they often find 15-20% reductions without effort. Cost to action: 2 hours.

**Supplier Renegotiation (3-4% savings)**
- You have leverage now. Call your top 3 suppliers (cleaning, equipment maintenance, music/towel services) with this line: *"I'm consolidating vendors — give me your best rate or I'm moving on."* Most will cut 10-15% to keep gym business. Spend 4 hours max making calls this month.

**Staffing Efficiency (2-3% savings — requires grit)**
- Map every staff hour against revenue generated (classes, PT sessions, member check-ins). Cut underutilised shifts or consolidate quiet periods. Be honest: is anyone delivering below-market value? If you're overstaffed in off-peak, a rota restructure saves real money. Don't slash — optimize.

**Skip Small Wins That Waste Time**
- Don't chase penny-pinching initiatives (cheaper coffee, bulk-buying towels). Focus only on the three above — they'll hit 10% without killing member experience or your sanity.

**Track Weekly**
- Create a one-page tracker. Review every Friday. Adjust immediately if targets slip.

What's your biggest current spend category — staffing, utilities,

# Updating Monthly Finance KPIs at The Forge

• **Pick 5 metrics max, track ruthlessly** — Lock in: membership churn rate, average revenue per member, class utilization %, payroll as % of revenue, and cash runway in months. Anything else dilutes focus. Update these on the 3rd of each month (right after month-end closes).

• **Link KPIs to *your* pain points, not spreadsheet theory** — If you're bleeding members, churn matters most right now. If cash flow is tight, track cash runway obsessively. If classes are half-empty, utilization %. Don't collect vanity metrics—track what actually moves your decisions.

• **Build a one-page dashboard, not a report** — 5 KPIs, actual number vs. target, simple red/yellow/green status. Share with yourself (discipline), any investors/partners (credibility), and your manager if you have one. Takes 30 mins to maintain.

• **Automate the boring bits immediately** — Pull membership data, attendance, and revenue automatically from your gym management software. Manual data entry = abandoned tracking. Spend your time *analyzing* the gap, not hunting numbers.

• **Action rule: One change per metric per month** — When a KPI dips, don't panic-fix everything. If churn spikes, run *one* retention experiment (e.g., exit interviews). Measure it next month. Prevents thrashing and shows what actually works.

What's your biggest current money problem—cash flow, member drop-off, or pricing power?

# Financial Forecasting Execution for The Forge

• **Pull 12 months of actual data TODAY** — revenue per member, churn rate, class attendance patterns, peak/off-peak seasons. Don't build forecasts on assumptions; Grace likely has this in her booking system already. This is the 2-hour foundation everything else sits on.

• **Model three scenarios, not one** — Conservative (10% growth), Base (your realistic target), Aggressive (if she nails referrals + new class offering). Plug in membership price increases at month 6-8 and new revenue streams (personal training, nutrition plans, corporate memberships). This forces clarity on what drives profit, not just vanity numbers.

• **Identify the 2-3 metrics that actually move the needle** — For a gym: probably member acquisition cost vs. lifetime value, and churn triggers (which months do women quit?). Track these weekly in a simple spreadsheet. Monthly financial statements are too slow for course-correction.

• **Set a hard rule: if actual revenue dips 15% below forecast by month 3, trigger a specific contingency** — e.g., "launch a member-get-member referral bonus" or "cut class times that underperform." Forecasting without action triggers is just busywork.

• **Use this to answer ONE decision right now** — Can she afford to hire a second PT this quarter, or should she wait until Q3? The forecast should make this binary question obvious, not debatable.

What's her current monthly revenue roughly running at?

kpis.Action: Map profitability of each service (classes vs PT vs add-ons) — build a simple per-service P&L
kpis.advice:
# Profitability Mapping for The Forge

**Do this today:**

• **Pull 90 days of actual data** — revenue from your booking system/till (class pack sales, PT session rates, add-ons like supplements/apparel). Don't estimate. Export whatever you have; messy data beats no data.

• **Assign direct costs ruthlessly** — per class: instructor pay + music/software licenses split across attendees. Per PT: trainer cut + any equipment wear. For add-ons: COGS only (what you paid for it). Don't allocate rent/utilities yet—that clouds the real picture.

• **Calculate contribution margin per service** — (Revenue - Direct Costs) ÷ Revenue = %. A 60% margin on PT is better than 20% on classes *if* you're pricing PT correctly. This is where your actual profit sits before overhead.

• **Rank by total profit dollars, not margin %** — classes run 15-20% margin but high volume may generate more absolute profit than PT. Know which service actually funds your gym.

• **Identify the leakage** — Which service underperforms? Kill low-margin, low-volume combos (e.g., if a 6am class runs half-full with 30% margins, that's a cash drain). Double down on your margin leader.

**This takes 3-4 hours max with a spreadsheet.** What's your highest-revenue service right now?

kpis.Action: Audit all active systemize automations — remove duplicates, confirm enquiry-to-trial flow works end to end.
kpis.advice:
# Audit & Fix Your Automations — Tactical Breakdown

• **Start with data forensics, not the dashboard.** Pull a CSV of every automation trigger you have running. Look for overlaps where the same enquiry might hit multiple workflows (e.g., email + SMS + Facebook messenger all firing for one new lead). Grace, this matters because duplicates tank your conversion rate AND annoy prospects. Use a spreadsheet to map: Trigger → Action → Outcome.

• **Run a live test with a dummy email address.** Don't assume your enquiry-to-trial flow works. Sign up as a prospect yourself through every channel (website form, Instagram DM, phone inquiry). Does she get a welcome sequence? Trial booking link? Follow-up reminder? Document what *actually* happens vs. what should happen. This takes 30 mins and catches real breakdowns.

• **Kill automations ruthlessly.** If you can't articulate why an automation exists in the next 60 seconds, delete it. Duplicates + dead workflows = confusion for staff + wasted time troubleshooting. Keep only: Welcome → Trial Booking Confirmation → Pre-Trial Prep → Post-Trial Follow-Up.

• **Assign one person (probably you) as "automation owner" going forward.** This isn't a one-time audit—it'll break again. Monthly check-in: does every automation still serve a purpose? Are new duplicates creeping in? 15-min sprint beats crisis-mode fixes later.

• **Test conversion velocity.** Time how long it takes from enquiry to trial booked. Target: under 24 hours. If it's longer, the bottleneck is your automation *sequence*, not the tech. Remove steps if

kpis.Action: Pick 3 repetitive tasks and build an AI automation
kpis.advice:
# AI Automation for The Forge — Concrete Action Plan

• **Task 1: Member onboarding emails** — Use Zapier + ChatGPT to auto-generate personalized welcome sequences triggered when someone books a trial. Template: fitness goals → class recommendations → facility tour details. This cuts your admin time from 45 mins/week to 5 mins. Start with 3 email templates (beginner/intermediate/experienced).

• **Task 2: Class booking reminders + no-show follow-ups** — Set up an automation that texts members 24h before classes, then auto-sends a "we missed you" message to no-shows with a rebook link. Use Mindbody API (if you're on that platform) or Twilio. Reduces no-shows by ~20% and you never send a manual reminder again.

• **Task 3: Social media content captions** — Record a 2-min voice memo of weekly wins (member testimonials, class updates, challenges completed), feed it to ChatGPT with a prompt like *"Turn this into 3 Instagram captions for women's fitness content — motivational tone, include a call-to-action"*. Generates 3 captions in 60 seconds instead of 20 minutes writing.

**Start here this week:** Pick whichever task annoys you most. Build it in one tool (Zapier is easiest). Test with 10 members. Measure time saved. Then scale the other two.

What's your current booking/CRM system?

kpis.Action: Start Cyber security for forge website
kpis.advice:
# Cybersecurity Action Plan for The Forge Website

• **Audit what you're actually protecting first** — Map every data touchpoint: member logins, payment info, class booking system, email lists. You can't secure what you don't know exists. Use a free tool like OWASP ZAP to scan for obvious vulnerabilities in 30 mins. This tells you what's actually broken vs. what's just "nice to have."

• **Get an SSL certificate live today** — If your site isn't HTTPS, do this immediately. Most hosts include this free now (GoDaddy, Wix, etc.). Takes 10 minutes. This encrypts member data in transit and kills your biggest liability with payment processing.

• **Hire one specific task, not a person** — Don't hire a cybersecurity consultant yet. Instead, pay a freelancer on Upwork (~£200-400) to do a focused "WordPress security hardening" or "Shopify security audit" (whatever platform you use). They'll lock down passwords, disable file editing, add backup automation. One-off cost, immediate results.

• **Set up automated backups + 2FA immediately** — Backups are your insurance policy if something breaks. 2-factor authentication on your admin account means even if someone steals your password, they can't access it. Both take 30 mins to configure and cost nothing.

• **Document one repeatable monthly task** — Have whoever manages the site spend 15 mins monthly checking for plugin updates and reviewing user access. Write it down so it gets done consistently.

**Bottom line:** You don't need a security overhaul—you need to plug the three biggest leaks. Do it this week.

marketing.advice:
**Insights**

Your cost-per-lead has ballooned 4.5x in four months (£6 → £27) whilst volume has collapsed 94% (69 → 4 leads). The close rate holding at 75% suggests creative and offer are still resonating with whoever *does* convert, but you're reaching almost nobody now. This isn't a conversion problem—it's a reach and relevance crisis. Meta's algorithm has likely throttled your ad set after months of declining performance; the platform sees diminishing returns and deprioritises spend. July's tiny budget (£109) was probably a reaction to panic, which makes the problem worse by starving the algorithm.

Your April performance (£6/lead, 60% close rate = £10 customer acquisition cost) was genuinely strong. You've been bleeding efficiency every single month since—a clear signal that creative fatigue, audience saturation, or targeting drift is killing performance.

**Actions**

1. **Audit and refresh creative immediately.** Pull the top 3-5 ad variations from April and May. Analyse what changed between then and now—copy angle, imagery, video hooks, CTA. Test 4-6 entirely new creative assets for August: shift the narrative away from whatever's been running (likely generic "join our gym" messaging). Try angles like member transformation stories, "women-only space" safety angle, specific class types (strength focus vs community vs flexibility), or pain-point driven (work stress, isolation, post-baby fitness). Video almost always outperforms static in fitness; if you're running stills, switch.

2. **Expand and rebuild your audience.** Your original targeting has likely been exhausted. Widen your interest stack (don't just target gym-goers; add wellness, mental health, women's empowerment, local community groups). Test a lookalike audience built from your April/May converters only (ignore the declining July cohort). Create a fresh cold audience segment: women 25-45 in Belfast + 5-mile radius interested in health/fitness but *not* currently engaged with gym ads. Exclude anyone who saw your ads in the last 60 days.

3. **Reset budget and scaling strategy.** July's £109 spend is too low to generate
